sections.heading: Problem Statement
sections.content: The test runner can already scope test runs to specific functions and modules via `validates` edges, but it can only do so when those edges exist. Currently `validates` edges come from two sources, both flawed: dFlow's `covers=` parameter requires manual annotation on a decorator (coupling test infrastructure to dFlow, drifting as tests evolve, and excluding projects that don't use dFlow), and manual `cortex link` commands (too tedious to maintain at scale). The test runner needs automatic, annotation-free `validates` edges generated by static AST analysis at scan time, with dFlow having no role in testâ†’code linking.

sections.heading: User Stories
sections.content:
**US-TR-01 â€” Run tests for a specific function**
As a developer, I want to run all tests that cover a specific function by naming that function so that I can validate a targeted change without running the full suite.

**US-TR-02 â€” Run tests for a module**
As a developer, I want to run all tests that cover any function in a module by naming that module so that I can validate a module-level refactor in one command.

**US-TR-03 â€” Store results for impact report**
As an agent, I want to run all tests associated with a function or module and have the results stored in the index so that test outcomes appear as verification evidence in the impact report.

**US-TR-04 â€” Automatic test-to-code linking**
As a developer, I want testâ†’code relationships to be detected automatically from the test source code so that I never need to maintain manual annotations or dFlow `covers=` parameters.

**US-TR-05 â€” No dFlow dependency for test linking**
As a developer, I want test coverage mapping to work on any Python project so that projects not using dFlow get the same scoped test runner behavior.

sections.heading: Current Capabilities
sections.content:
| Capability | Status |
|---|---|
| `cortex test` command | Done |
| Runner detection (pixi / poetry / plain pytest) | Done |
| JUnit XML report parsing | Done |
| pytest-json-report parsing (preferred) | Done |
| `--node <node_id>` scoping via `validates` edges | Done |
| `--path <prefix>` scoping via module prefix | Done |
| `--no-save` flag | Done |
| `test_runs` and `test_results` tables | Done |
| Unmapped test flagging in summary output | Done |
| `validates` edges from dFlow `covers=` parameter | Done |
| AST call graph `validates` edges (no dFlow dependency) | Done |
| Migration path off dFlow `covers=` | Not started |

sections.heading: AST Call Graph Design
sections.content:
The call graph scanner replaces dFlow `covers=` as the source of `validates` edges. It runs as part of the module scanner pipeline during `cortex build`.

**How it works:**

1. Detect test files by convention (`test_*.py`, `*_test.py`) or by the presence of pytest markers.
2. For each test function in the file, build an import nameâ†’module map from the file's `import` and `from â€¦ import` statements.
3. Walk the function body AST, collecting all `Call` nodes (direct function calls only â€” no transitive traversal).
4. For each call, resolve the called name against the import map to get a qualified module path (e.g. `from pm.method import execute` + call `execute(...)` â†’ `pm.method.execute`).
5. Look up the resolved qualified name as a cortex node ID. If found â†’ emit a `validates` edge from the test node to the production node. If not found (stdlib, third-party, unresolvable) â†’ silently skip.

**What this handles correctly:**

| Pattern | Result |
|---|---|
| `from pm.method import execute; execute(...)` | Edge created |
| `import pm.method; pm.method.execute(...)` | Edge created (attribute access on import alias) |
| `numpy.max(...)` | Silently ignored â€” not in cortex index |
| Parameterized test calling multiple functions | All resolvable calls get edges |
| Fixture-mediated call (`client.execute()`) | No edge â€” type unknown without inference |
| Dynamic dispatch (`getattr(obj, fn)()`) | No edge â€” unresolvable statically |

**Scope:** direct calls only. Transitive coverage (test â†’ A â†’ B â†’ C) is what runtime tools like `pytest-cov` provide. Static call graph edges represent what the test *directly* exercises, which is the right scope for focused test runs.

**dFlow migration:** once call graph edges are live, `covers=` on `@test_workflow` becomes redundant. Running `cortex build` after the feature ships auto-generates equivalent edges. dFlow can remove `covers=` from its decorator API in a subsequent release.

sections.heading: Backlog
sections.content:
~~**AST call graph `validates` edges**~~ -- Completed. Implemented in module_scanner via enriched `name_map` and `_collect_functions`. See the Call Graph Design section above for the original specification.

**Fixture-mediated call resolution** â€” Tests that exercise production code through a pytest fixture (e.g. `def test_x(client): client.execute()`) cannot be resolved by static AST without type inference. V1 accepts this gap: such tests are unmapped and flagged in the summary. Manual `cortex link` is the escape hatch.

**`covers=` migration tooling** â€” A one-time `cortex migrate-covers` command that reads existing dFlow `covers=` annotations and emits the equivalent `cortex link` commands, allowing projects to audit the mapping before deleting the dFlow annotations.

**Transitive coverage queries** â€” A read-only query (not edge generation): given a node, which tests transitively cover it by following the call graph? Useful for understanding the full test surface before a deep refactor. Distinct from the direct-call edges used for test scoping.
